Beth-el was forgotten. But as many as God loves, he will remind them of neglected duties, one way or another, by conscience or by events. When we have vowed a vow to God, it is best not to delay fulfilling it; yet better late than never. Jacob instructed his household to prepare, not only for the journey and relocation, but also for religious services. Heads of families should use their authority to maintain religion in their families. They must put away false gods. In families where there appears to be a devotion to God and an altar is present, yet often much is amiss, and more false gods than one would suppose are present. They must cleanse themselves and change their clothes. These were outward ceremonies signifying the purifying and renewal of the heart. What are clean clothes and new clothes without a clean heart and a renewed heart? If Jacob had requested these idols earlier, they would have parted with them sooner. Sometimes attempts at reform succeed better than we could have thought. Jacob buried their images. We must be completely separated from our sins, as we are from those who are dead and buried out of sight. He moved from Shechem to Beth-el. Although the Canaanites were very angry against the sons of Jacob for their brutal treatment of the people of Shechem, they were restrained by God’s power so that they could not take advantage of the opportunity to avenge them. The way of duty is the way of safety. When we are engaged in God's work, we are under special protection; God is with us while we are with him; and if He is for us, who can be against us? God governs the world more by secret terrors on people’s minds than we realize.

The comfort that saints have in sacred services does not come so much from Beth-el, the house of God, as from El-beth-el, the God of the house. The services are empty things if we do not meet with God in them. There Jacob buried Deborah, Rebekah’s nurse. She died and was greatly mourned. Old servants in a family who have been faithful and useful should be respected. God appeared to Jacob. He renewed the covenant with him. I am God Almighty, God all-sufficient, able to fulfill the promise in due time, and to support you and provide for you in the meantime. Two things are promised: that he would be the father of a great nation, and that he would possess a good land. These two promises had a spiritual meaning, which Jacob understood somewhat, though not as clearly and distinctly as we do now. Christ is the promised Seed, and heaven is the promised land; the former is the foundation, and the latter the crowning glory, of all God’s blessings.

Rachel passionately said, Give me children, or else I die; and now that she had children, she died! The death of the body is only the departure of the soul to the spirit world. When will we learn that it is God alone who truly knows what is best for his people, and that in all worldly matters the safest path for the Christian is to say from the heart, It is the Lord; let him do as he pleases. Here alone is our safety and comfort, to know no will but his. Her dying lips called her newborn son Ben-oni, the son of my sorrow; and many a son proves to be the grief of his mother. Children are often the sorrow of their mothers; they should, therefore, when they grow up, strive to be their joy, and so, if possible, make amends. But Jacob, because he did not want to constantly remind himself of the mother’s death each time he called his son, changed his name to Benjamin, the son of my right hand: that is, very dear to me; the support of my old age, like a staff in my right hand.

What a severe affliction Reuben's sin was is shown by the statement, “and Israel heard it.” No more is said, but that is enough. Reuben thought that his father would never hear about it; but those who expect to keep their sins secret are generally disappointed. The age and death of Isaac are recorded, even though he died not until after Joseph was sold into Egypt. Isaac lived approximately forty years after he made his will. We shall not die an hour sooner, but much the better, if we prepare our hearts and homes in advance. Particular notice is taken of the agreement between Esau and Jacob at their father’s funeral to show how God had wonderfully changed Esau's mind. It is appalling to see relatives sometimes disputing over the graves of their friends for a little of this world’s possessions, while they themselves are nearing death.
